item.title: I'm so mad
item.content:encoded: Okay. This is so good. And I'm mad because I wanted to make an SCP based on this planet and you did it better than I would have. I literally haven't stopped thinking about it since I read it. Take my +1.
Holy balls

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I understand this is a Schroedinger's Cat situation, I just don't quite understand why this particularly scenario has put the weight of that burden on the Foundation. Whatever this planet's memetic defense system is, it seems to implant the Schroedinger's Cat dilemma into the observers head indirectly.

Even if this planet's inability to be observed is too dangerous for continued study, it seems to un-Foundation like to buy into an SCP's effect on people and be as dramatic as suggesting observation may equate to genocide. I think the idea is good, but I think it be more believable if the choice to not observe is because anyone attempting research is unusually haunted by the possibilities of finding the truth.

I understand that might be to big a change to your article as this point, it makes the article less meta-textual and more psychological.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like Aequitus as a name. If I ever write something where I need to give it a name beyond Planet Nine/SCP-4774, I'm using this one :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Its astronomy shit, so i already LOVE IT
One of my favorite Series V articles gets a well deserved 200 upvotes!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aequitus.
This is Roman word for Balance, and judging by this SCP article alone, it's basically Shrodingers planet. Is it alive or dead? Is it responsible for being or responsible for lacking? Why not both, to make it Perfectly balanced?
As all things should be

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's Schrodinger's Planet. Basically, some sort of anomalous effect makes the planet difficult to observe or study. The possibility that life may exist there also includes the possibility that it may not, and observation would lead to discovering one of those two possibilities.

For some reason related to its effects, the Foundation has decided that intentionally leaving the existence of life on this planet as theoretical is a way of preserving that life, as opposed to further investigation that would reveal they don't/can't exist which the Foundation views as tantamount to genocide of this potential life.

That was my surface take on it anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one kinda makes my head hurt. Not because of the SCP itself, but what it'd be named if it existed. What are interesting Roman Mythology figures we haven't used yet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It’s skips like these that remind me why this site is so cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
schrodinger's planet

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's certainly a very interesting idea for an SCP here, but I feel as though the idea that the Foundation would choose to simply not learn anything more about a potential anomaly just because of the hypothetical existence of life that may or may not be sapient seems really out of character to me.

As said, the idea is very promising, but that one detail somewhat makes the idea fall flat, especially since that seems to be the intended biggest detail about the whole thing.

-1 for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cant wait to see more world building!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the attention to immense detail as you progressed through the article, since it’s common for articles to just lose their flair as it goes, this one just kept it up and going. Love the whole hypothetical thing you go going on. Overall great article :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Its quite rare to find a SCP using that third part of the motto as its focus. (Secure. Contain. Protect.) This is a good example of that idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the first time I found some reference to the Dark sun Nibiru in the SCP archives and frankly im suprised.

Writing is a bit empty, being devoid of solid facts.
That said, this IS part of a larger canon, which I will shortly start reading.
I may come back to this, but i already +1'd so I might not.

Either way, +1

item.title: Re: The Tenth Planet?
item.content:encoded: Jesus, no wonder the old series of Who made kids hide behind the sofa. That's far more terrifying than the modernised Cybermen, even though it is essentially just a repurposed fireproof undersuit and some random bits from a plumbing and kitchenware store.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Blimey, that's meta. Essentially, by considering the possibility of a ninth (true) planet, and the civilisation that may or may not exist upon it, we're both creating and potentially destroying it?

I wonder if really whether SCP-4774 is the planet itself, or the concept of said planet (and its hypothetical inhabitants). Like, some kind of memetic virus which, never mind its effect of collapsing the hyperpositional waveform of the planet - and its people via a Morton's fork into inevitable nonexistence - could well drive the astronomical and maybe wider scientific and philosophical community crazy…

Though, is it absolutely a given that if we found a super-Jupiter gas giant out around 700AU, it would necessarily be devoid of life, even an intelligent civilisation? Jupiter itself is warmer than can be explained by mere solar heating alone, so the extreme, intra-Kuiper/Oort distance from the sun need not be an energetic barrier.

…or am I now falling into another memetic trap?

Derp, super-Earth, not super-Jupiter. In fact, something roughly around the size of (ahem) Uranus. So whilst it may still have some internal heating, it'd be nowhere near as much as Jupiter's. Still, it could host life as we don't know it. But then, failing to consider that possibility is presumably part of its memetic effects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I HATED this as it seemed like "there is no anomaly and the Foundation are stupid taking their paranoia to new highs.

Then I reread it.

Subjects that don't know what a planet is still come to the same conclusions? ok so that is a legit anomaly. These are normal thoughts for astrologists and wouldn't seem odd. but for a 5 year old to start discussing "hypothetical existence" I am sold.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Firstly, kudos for making an anomaly out of an actual real-world thing/question (and I don't just mean taking pictures or historical context). Secondly, kudos for making it believable. Thirdly, you managed to make an SCP that has infiltrated the public and is effectively uncontainable and has infected the Foundation, while also making it not XK-related. And thirdly, I'm a sucker for min screws. +1, and more if I could.

item.title: Not My Problem?
item.content:encoded:
Its not my problem right? So it's not there?
This is well written, and deserves its rating. +1

Gosh, a milestone already. I think this might've been my fastest yet, excluding one anomaly, and it's really surprising— when I posted this, I never expected it to do particularly well. But I also know that I loved the idea, and it's really, truly gratifying to see that other people love it as well. Thank you all <3

item.title: Re: The Tenth Planet?
item.content:encoded:
Aha. I knew it, it's secretly The Tenth Planet. You cyberfolk tryna cover up your operations by calling it the ninth planet instead of the Tenth.

Im on to you!

item.title: Re: The Tenth Planet?
item.content:encoded: I can neither confirm nor deny the presence of existentially ambiguous cybermen on SCP-4774.

item.title: The Tenth Planet?
item.content:encoded:
I read the title and immediately thought of these guys-

No uncollapsed images on the forums, please. -Zyn
hide

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this kinda reminds me of hitchhikers guide t0o the galaxy definitely gonna +1 cause i love that book

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
don't think about it
you'll break something by thinking about it
+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Further information on the anomaly can be obtained by considering SCP-4774.

This is the best ending I've ever seen on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
block SCP-4774 from view

Since the description of the anomaly is largely based on hypothesis .. I guess the planet is not visible?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Once we get the hub set up and the important, tone-setting worldbuilding done, the canon will be open to relevant contributions from anyone, as all (most?) canons are.

(I suppose there's also no way to stop people from writing for it now and saying they want to put the thing on the hub, but as a group we're trying to keep things streamlined to organize the universe before opening the doors, so to speak.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP Foundation takes to space along with a large portion of the rest of humanity

I'm already intrigued. How do I contributee?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Disclaimer: This is part of a larger canon, titled Ad Astra Per Aspera

And our hub will be up as soon as we get a little more of this worldbuilding done!

Great job on this one, MaliceAF.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this article.

The special containment procedures seem a bit harsh, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Clever unfurling of the premise. I really like where you take this.

Also, nice usage of good old SEP. Glad it's continuing to be a thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If Philip K. Dick and Robert Anton Wilson had collaborated, they would have been pleased to have come up with this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
VIVA LA NOT-PLUTO, B!

Take my +1, I love conceptual skips and this just does everything for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is excellent. The idea of partial non-existence as an evolutionary adaptation to an inhospitable environment is the kind of inspired, almost-sensical idea that makes me love SCPs. You can't conceptualize non-existence as a trait that could be evolved, but it makes just enough sense that, in the domain of the anomalous, you can believe it. Who knows, maybe it does make sense and your understanding is just incomplete?
The second set of conclusions gave me mild chills due to the implication.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i am confuse

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting stuff, but I feel that it ends quite abruptly. I get that the reason it can't be further expanded upon is part of the narrative, but that aspect leads to the artcle feeling insubstantial - at least for me, personally.

Very tentative novote, though I'm curious to see how you'll expand upon this in Ad Astra.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. It hits on a bunch of themes that I find interesting, and the idea of the Foundation considering alien sapients to be equal to humans is an interesting one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I totally have no bias to ad astra per aspera content definitely not

Space is cool. Weird conceptual/ontological stuff is cool. Exploring space is double cool. Verdict: I like this a lot. That last line is also especially great.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now this is the way to do a proper mind-break

+1

also more space skips yay

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to 9Volt, Weryllium, MrBazzle, Cyantreuse, stormbreath, Drblackbox, and Lord_of_Laugh for looking this over before posting. Hope you all enjoy a mind-screw about hypothetical aliens.

Disclaimer: This is part of a larger canon, titled Ad Astra Per Aspera, in which the SCP Foundation takes to space along with a large portion of the rest of humanity. Earth is slowly rendered uninhabitable, and they're forced to forge a new life among the stars. That's the reason the Foundation has FTL travel, why they have such control over the acts of civilians, and why they're unconcerned about the veil breaking — within the context of the larger setting, the world at large is already aware of anomalies.
